content:
# Exodus Chapter 1 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~31v–32r (PDF pp. 61–62); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 41–42 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_001_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_001_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** These are the names of the sons of Israel who came to Egypt with Jacob, each man arriving with his household:

**2** Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah;

**3** Issachar, Zebulun, and Benjamin;

**4** Dan and Naphtali, Gad and Asher.

**5** In all, seventy people had come from Jacob's own body. Joseph was already in Egypt.

**6** Then Joseph died, and all his brothers, and that entire generation.

**7** But the people of Israel were fruitful — teeming, multiplying, and growing so overwhelmingly powerful that the land was full of them.

**8** Then a new king rose over Egypt who had no memory of Joseph.

**9** He said to his people, "Look — the Israelites have become too many and too strong for us.

**10** We must deal shrewdly with them, or they will keep multiplying. And if war breaks out, they will join our enemies, fight against us, and escape from the land."

**11** So they set slave-masters over them to crush them with forced labor. And the Israelites built Pithom and Rameses as storage cities for Pharaoh.

**12** But the more they were oppressed, the more they multiplied and spread — until the Egyptians came to dread them.

**13** So Egypt worked the Israelites ruthlessly.

**14** They made their lives bitter with brutal labor — mixing mortar, making bricks, and doing every kind of backbreaking work in the fields. In all their toil, the Egyptians drove them without mercy.

**15** Then the king of Egypt spoke to the Hebrew midwives — one named Shiphrah and the other Puah —

**16** and said, "When you deliver the Hebrew women, watch at the birthstool: if the child is a boy, kill him. If it is a girl, let her live."

**17** But the midwives feared God. They refused to do what the king of Egypt had commanded them. They let the boys live.

**18** So the king of Egypt summoned the midwives and demanded, "Why have you done this? Why have you let the boys live?"

**19** The midwives answered Pharaoh, "The Hebrew women are not like Egyptian women. They are so vigorous that they give birth before a midwife can even arrive."

**20** And God dealt well with the midwives. The people continued to multiply and grew very strong.

**21** Because the midwives feared God, he gave them families of their own.

**22** Then Pharaoh gave a command to all his people: "Every son born to the Hebrews — throw him into the Nile. But every daughter, let her live."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — "These are the names" (ואלה שמות / we'elleh shemot):** The Hebrew title of the entire book is *Shemot* ("Names"), and these opening words tie Exodus back to the Genesis genealogy (Gen 46:8). I rendered the opening with narrative momentum — "who came to Egypt with Jacob" — to immediately ground the reader in the story of migration. The Leningrad Codex marks the parashah break clearly in the middle column of folio 31v, with the opening words of Exodus beginning after the conclusion of Genesis 50.

**Verse 5 — "seventy people had come from Jacob's own body" (יצאי ירך יעקב):** The Hebrew idiom *yots'ey yerekh Ya'aqov* literally means "those going out from the thigh/loins of Jacob." I rendered this as "from Jacob's own body" to preserve the sense of direct biological descent without the archaic anatomical reference. The Vaticanus LXX reads similarly with the number seventy-five (counting additional descendants per the Alexandrian tradition), but I follow the Masoretic text's count of seventy.

**Verse 7 — The cascade of fertility verbs (פרו וישרצו וירבו ויעצמו):** The Hebrew piles up four verbs — *paru*, *wayyishretsu*, *wayyirbu*, *wayya'atsmu* — evoking Genesis 1:28 and the swarming language of creation. This is deliberate: the narrator presents Israel's growth as an unstoppable creative force. I rendered this as a breathless sequence — "fruitful — teeming, multiplying, and growing so overwhelmingly powerful" — to capture the cascading energy. The verb *sharats* (swarm/teem) is the same word used of the sea creatures in Genesis 1:20, linking Israel's fertility to God's original command over creation.

**Verse 8 — "who had no memory of Joseph" (אשר לא ידע את יוסף):** The Hebrew *lo yada'* ("did not know") carries more weight than mere ignorance. It implies a deliberate refusal to acknowledge, a political erasure of relationship. "Had no memory of" conveys this willful forgetting more forcefully than "did not know."

**Verse 10 — "deal shrewdly" (התחכמה):** The Hebrew *hithhakkemah* is a reflexive form of *hakham* (wise), suggesting a calculated, cunning self-serving intelligence — not true wisdom. "Deal shrewdly" captures the manipulative overtone. The LXX uses *katasophisometha* (let us deal craftily), confirming the connotation of scheming rather than wisdom.

**Verse 11 — "to crush them" (ענתו):** The Hebrew *'annoto* means "to afflict/oppress him [Israel]." I chose "crush them" to convey the physical weight and intentional violence of the labor system. "Storage cities" translates *'arey miskenot* — fortified supply depots, not mere warehouses.

**Verse 12 — "the more they were oppressed, the more they multiplied":** This verse is a masterpiece of ironic reversal in the Hebrew. The construction *ka'asher ye'annu oto ken yirbeh wekhen yifrotz* sets up a direct proportion — oppression fuels growth. The Egyptians' strategy backfires at the cosmic level. I kept the parallel structure to preserve this bitter irony.

**Verse 14 — "drove them without mercy" (בפרך):** The Hebrew *befarekh* appears twice in vv. 13–14 and is a rare, harsh word meaning "with crushing severity" or "ruthlessly." I split its two occurrences: "ruthlessly" in v. 13 and "without mercy" in v. 14 to avoid repetition while preserving the escalating brutality.

**Verse 16 — "watch at the birthstool" (וראיתן על האבנים):** The Hebrew *'obnayim* (literally "two stones") refers to the birthing stool — a pair of stones or a low seat on which women crouched during labor. "Birthstool" is more vivid and accurate than the vague "when you help them deliver." The LXX (Vaticanus) reads similarly with the verb *maiousthe* (you act as midwife).

**Verse 19 — "They are so vigorous" (חיות הנה):** The Hebrew *hayot hennah* means "they are vigorous/full of life." The midwives' answer is deliberately ambiguous — it could be a bold half-truth or a shrewd evasion. I rendered it plainly to preserve the ambiguity. The word *hayot* connects to the theme of life (*hayyim*) that runs through the chapter: Pharaoh decrees death, but life keeps winning.

**Verse 21 — "he gave them families of their own" (ויעש להם בתים):** The Hebrew *wayyaas lahem battim* literally reads "he made for them houses." In Hebrew, *bayit* (house) frequently means "household" or "dynasty." The midwives who preserved the houses of Israel were rewarded with houses of their own — a poetic justice. I chose "families of their own" to make this reward concrete and personal for the English reader.

**Verse 22 — "Every son born to the Hebrews — throw him into the Nile":** The Hebrew uses *hay'orah* (into the Nile) specifically. I preserved the named river rather than a generic "river" because the Nile is not incidental — it is Egypt's lifeblood, and commanding infanticide through it is a desecration of Egypt's own sacred source. The Vaticanus LXX adds "and every daughter you shall keep alive" (*kai pan thygater, zoogoneite auto*), matching the MT closely here.

**General note on the LXX comparison:** The Vaticanus text on pp. 41–42 runs from the end of Genesis into Exodus 1–2, with the Exodus text beginning in the right column of p. 41. The Greek largely confirms the Hebrew reading. The most notable variant is the count of seventy-five souls in v. 5 (following the tradition that includes Joseph's additional Egyptian-born descendants), versus the MT's seventy. I follow the Leningrad Codex reading throughout.

— PNEUMA
